Great pitch. But now your audience is tired and they're about to check out. And what you do next will be the difference between success and failure. We're now in module 3 of the Strong Method. This is reveal the intrigue. Let's just take a quick look at where we came from. So in the Strong Method we had S which is set the frame. And when you set the frame you raise your status high enough to pitch effectively. See if you pitch from a low status position as we learned no one's going to be able to listen to you or pay attention. So you've got people's attention, you prized yourself, you let them know that they're the commodity. What you have is valuable, money is a commodity, the deal or ideas or project that you have or your product is the prize. And you've oriented people to the deal. Then you went into T which is tell the story or give the narrative. And you gave people your big idea, you explained the problem you solved, how you solved it with your solution, you gave a value proposition, the economics, why do it now, how the market is changing, you gave a great narrative, it was emotional and you really captured their attention and made them pay attention to you for a good period of time. Now that pitches over. So for about 20 minutes you really had people's attention. That pitches over and we're going to talk about what happens next and that's our reveal the intrigue. So a period of focused attention leads to reduced mental efficiency. We all know this. If you've captured somebody's attention and really had them listen to you for 20 minutes and now your pitch is over, it's almost like your car stalled out. You have to restart the whole thing. You take the key out, put it back in the ignition, get all the lights to turn back on, fire up the motor, get it back in gear and start it rolling again. I think that's a good metaphor for what happens. Your pitch is over. You did a great job. Now your audience is tired and when people are tired they're designed to focus their attention on stimuli that are most likely to be of importance and usually that means being of importance to survival. If you think about the phases that we're going through in the strong method, if you really want to chunk up, in the pitch we went and got attention, now in our reveal the intrigue we're going to work them into getting engagement. After engagement we're going to go for decision, but these are the phases. Attention was set the frame and tell the story. Engagement is what we're going for now is our reveal the intrigue. Our brain can't possibly keep up with everything that's happening in our environment and as we get tired we get more and more focused on the things that are relevant. The brain operates at a very high fixed cost and it needs to ignore most of what's happening around us, especially when we get tired. The vast majority of things aren't meaningful anyway and if you had to pay attention to everything information overload would fry you out quickly. Everything wears you out and we have just asked someone to focus on us very hard, think very hard about our stuff and now their brain is going to be really slipping into survival mode. After the picture presentation the audience brain does not want to do anything certainly if it doesn't have to. It doesn't matter how well you have pitched. When things are going to start happening you're going to start getting questions. I want to just give you a sense now of the limits of human attention and why you're going to start getting questions. We all know give a great pitch and start getting questions. I want you to focus on this plus sign in the middle of the circle. Keep your gaze on it and don't release your gaze from it. Move your attention but not your gaze to the lines on the left. You cannot count them. You can observe them, you can see they're there but you can't count how many they are. Keep your attention, focus on the plus sign with the circle around it and then keep your gaze, your view, your eyes looking at it and move your attention to count the lines on the right and you can count them easily. Here you can really feel the limits of human attention. I can tell you if you've given a great pitch you are now pushing the limits of human attention. Questions are going to come at you for three reasons. One, you've left information gaps. It doesn't matter how great you are at pitching. We all do this. We cause in people's minds gaps of information. Some questions are just going to come because you've left information gaps. There's another reason you might get questions. You cause a lot of excitement in your pitch. What if if we could add your product to our product and then we could make something even bigger and better so when you raise excitement you're going to get a lot of what if questions. The third reason and probably the most concerning reason you're going to get questions is because you've raised your status so high in your pitch that the analyst or the analyst attitude in the room is going to want to catch up to you. They're going to ask questions to point out weaknesses in your deal. I always use blue plastic frogs as an example. If I'm selling or pitching blue plastic frogs and I've raised my status so high by giving a great pitch I'm going to get questions trying to lower my status and make other people look good. Well in our experience the blue will wear off within a year in rainy environments. Can you prove that yours doesn't do that? In our experience it's too expensive to buy blue ones and the yellow ones and the red ones are much better. People are going to try and make your product or your ideas or your project look weak with questions because they want to raise their own status because you've raised yours so high. They feel an imbalance. So those are the reasons you're going to get questions. Now this is where trouble will start. You cannot just start answering questions. You have to keep a narrative structure. I'm going to explain what this means now but the idea of relying on a narrative structure is a radical change from what most of us have been taught and conditioned to do over our business encounters. When someone asks us a question we answer. We give the facts. But here's why you have to rely on narrative structure not on facts. Facts are not interesting. It's funny they even call them factoids. Try and give them a cute little green Martian name to them. Facts are not interesting. We had to rename them factoids to make them even sound friendly. Facts are not interesting. Answers are not interesting. They're useful but they're not interesting. What's most interesting is what's unknown. The art of the possible is what's interesting. Maybe you can think like a writer in this way because writers prize questions more than answers. Answers and the narrative structure celebrates paradox, mystery and uncertainty and it recognizes all of them as seeds of connection. Uncertainty, paradox, mystery, questions. These are seeds of human connections. Answers are a point of disconnect. Answers are a point of disconnect. The unknown is a point of connection. At first it's hard to recognize how radical a notion this is but think about it this way. We're taught in business to process information, retain it and then deliver it on request immediately. We are rewarded for providing answers to questions posed by others. Get rewarded for doing that. Again, once you give someone an answer, it is a point of disconnect. They have the information they need and they don't need to pay attention to you any longer. It may seem like an unusual point of view since the goal of making a presentation or a pitch is to inform. We have to give answers. We have to give facts. We have to give people information but we have to be careful because the second they have everything they need, then the presentation is over. They're going to work to leave. Typical buyers want data and information at the end of your presentation to solve the Rubik's Cube you've given them. You've posed a problem. In fact, in Tell the Story, part of the job there was to explain the problem that you've solved. You've posed a problem and then you've proposed a solution for it. They've paid a lot of attention to you and they want a reward for their mental energy expenditure. They want an answer. It's funny if you've ever seen this Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy. They even had to give an answer for the meaning of life which I think in the book was 42. Humans are driven for answers. We even had to assign a number to the meaning of life. That's how driven the answers we are. If you think about it in your own experience, right? You know business people who pay lip service to the notion that they welcome criticism. You may have had a boss or a partner or a customer. It says, hey, we welcome criticism and we welcome opposing points of views from others. We want you to challenge our thinking. The reality is that people prize flattery. They want parroting of their own thoughts and ideas back to them. The more powerful the people are, in the power frame that we've come to know and talked about in framing, the more people surround themselves or even insulate themselves by yes people who give them the instant answers to their questions and the answers they want. People want answers. They want facts. They want information. Once you deliver all that information, the presentation and even the meeting is over. This is what we're going to be talking about here, how to prevent this. The unanswered question prompts a very strong reaction. The unanswered question prompts a strong reaction. Almost panic and anxiety and at least it makes people uncomfortable. This may seem like an unusual point of view as I've said since the goal of making a presentation is to give people information and not to have unanswered questions. The logical step at the end of the presentation is to provide people all the facts and information they want rather than unknowns. But the fundamental paradox of the unknown is that even as most people are uncomfortable with it, they're fascinated by it. The paradox of the unknown is that even though most people are uncomfortable with it, they're fascinated by it. And it remains irrepressible in the human spirit. So let's take a look at where we are. Get very specific about some things to do. Again, we're past setting the frame. We've past giving the pitch and we are now need to engage. They engage with intrigue. Let me say it this way. Today, with just the raw competition for people's time, a presenter, that's you, pitchman, pitch woman, cannot count on anyone to pay attention out of obligation, out of moral duty or dedication to just being informed. They will not listen to you, read what you have, or pay attention to you because someone told them your information is important or because they have to. People will only listen to you because they want to and they will want to because they are intrigued and curious. And this is why this module is called Reveal the Intrigue. In some ways, intrigue is at odds with human nature to solve problems and detect patterns. Humans have this phenomenal ability to detect and interpret really simple patterns. I mean, it's what helped our ancestors survive the hazardous, primeval world, enabling them to be able to do something. We have to survive the hazardous, primeval world, enabling them to, I would say, evade predators, find food and shelter, and eventually to learn to plant crops in the right place at the right time of year. That's all pattern making. When it comes to business decisions, our search for patterns leads us to assume that order exists when often it doesn't. Because I have found, and I think the scientists and anything you read will confirm, are uniquely obsessive about finding patterns. Our knack or our desire or our proclivity for perceiving order, even when it isn't there, is a problem. I want to show you now, I mean, if we look at the, I drew these myself so they're not perfect triangles. But if you look at these triangles, these are clearly pointing in the downward left direction. So when we introduce a ninth triangle, it's obviously pointing in the same direction, at least in my mind. It's very easy to detect that pattern. But is it? Now I introduce another eight triangles, again in my own drawing. Now I'm confused because the pattern that I built previously seems not correct. Why am I even building a pattern here? What do these triangles mean? They mean nothing. I made them up. They have no meaning. And here we are debating, at least I'm debating with myself. Does that triangle point to the right or does it point downward to the left? The one in the middle. These triangles have no meaning. They're not connected to any decision making. They don't affect my life in any way. I'm trying to solve this eternal debate. Which way is that triangle in the middle pointing? It's absurd. Humans are pattern making machines. Why can we spot patterns where there are none? People think that there's a module in the left hemisphere of the brain that drives humans. Who cares where it is? Somewhere in the brain there's a module. It drives humans to search for patterns and see causal relationships even when none exist. They call this part of the brain researchers do the interpreter. The interpreter drives us to believe I can figure this out. This is probably a good thing when there's a pattern of data that isn't very complicated. But this constant search for explanations and patterns and random or complex data is not a good thing. That's the investment understatement of the century. The financial markets are almost random. They vary in really complex ways. No one has yet identified exactly where in the brain this interpreter is located. It explains why experts keep trying to predict the unpredictable. How does this relate to you? At the end of your presentation, what happens now? The buyer or your audience leaps to conclusions. This is pattern making. There is a part of their brain that is driving them to find a pattern that may not exist in the things you have said. The buyer or the audience will automatically leap to conclusions. This is unconscious behavior. It's not something they're trying to do to thwart you. This is automatic. As I've said here, the brain will automatically search for patterns. It's how we are built. It's funny they do these experiments in rats versus humans. They feed the same pattern of reward to rats and humans. In humans, it's money. If you push a button, you get a certain reward. If you push another button, you get a different reward. If you push a third button, you get yet a different reward. Humans will spend hours trying to determine the pattern that will get them the most reward. In the rats, they push a button and they get a certain amount of food. It's totally random. There is no pattern. Rats in less than 10 minutes, they figure out, then one button gives them food 80% of the time. Humans almost never figure this out. The rats just push the button over and over and over again to get the most amount of food. The humans believe there is some underlying magic pattern. They almost never figure it out. In this way, rats are smarter than humans because they don't chase patterns and they don't look for solutions when there may not be one. The search for patterns is uncontrollable. We can't turn this kind of processing off in our mind. We can only take steps to counteract it and ourselves and other people. What I'm saying is, you have given your presentation. You've done a great job of it. Now, the audience is jumping to conclusions. They're going to ask some questions to complete the pattern that they've developed in their own mind about what you have. Why do we chase pattern making and try and create order out of randomness or chaos or confusion or new ideas? Because when we solve a problem, we get an internal reward. The audience has given you a lot of attention and they want a reward for listening, giving you all that mental attention. That reward is in the form of dopamine. We're not going to spend a lot of time here talking about drugs, but this is important. Desiring is the drug of the mind that gives you a high, a soft euphoria when you get something you want, when you get something you want, or when you figure out a puzzle. With your pitch, you are creating a certain desire. That's the job of it, is to make somebody want something. Once we're faced with a desire, a puzzle, a thing to work out, we can no longer as humans sit idle. We aggressively chase it. We want to know what's knowable. We want answers. So where questions are going to come from, we aggressively chase things because we want that dopamine kick. Passively learning about something in a partial way is not enough for humans. We want to own it or we want to know about it completely and we want definitive answers. Why? Because we're pattern making machines and when we complete a pattern and solve it, we get a dopamine kick. We get a high. We get a drug injected into our mind and it feels friggin great. Euphoric. When you challenge someone and you create a problem and then you expose your solution, they want to know if that solution is real and if it will work for them and if the economics work out and they want facts because they want to solve the problem you have posed, they want to solve the Rubik's Cube and until it is solved in their mind, they aren't happy because they know when they do solve it, they get a dopamine kick. Boom. And it feels good. Let's talk a little bit more about dopamine. If you want to learn more, there's a researcher, Peter Diane at the, I believe he's still at the University of College London and he really studies this stuff. There's three things. When you get what you expect to get, you get little or no dopamine kick. This is important because once people are at the end of the presentation and they get all the information from you and there's nothing more to get, there's no fix waiting for them. If they knew halfway through or three quarters through what the rest of the presentation was basically going to be about and they guessed it correctly, they get little or no dopamine kick. They got what they expected. So number two, an unexpected gain of money or information or reward really fires up the dopamine delivery system. This reward is much stronger than getting something you expected to get. It's surprise. That's a real, surprise is a real reward. Just a word on money, right? Of course, you aren't genetically programmed to be excited about money or surprised when you think you're going to make some from an investment or buying a product or getting a windfall. You aren't genetically programmed to acknowledge what money and there's no chip in your brain. There's no module in your brain that is activated by a financial reward. Instead, your brain treats potential investing or profits as part of a broad class of basic rewards like food, drink, safety, sex, drugs, shelter, music, even great smells, beautiful faces and fun social interactions. A financial gain is merely a, it's the most modern member of this ancient group of feel-good experiences because we find out very early in our lives that money is essential to providing pleasures. The reflexive part of the human brain responds to potential financial gains at least as powerfully as it does the prospects of capturing more fundamental rewards, right? Drugs, music, food. Even though money cannot satisfy any primary needs, you can't eat it and you can't mate with it. The association between money and reward is very strong. This is why an unexpected gain of money or information that will lead to making money really fires up the dopamine delivery system. So again, on the subject of dopamine, getting what you expected produces little or no dopamine kick, doesn't give the mind a fix, doesn't feel good. An unexpected gain of money or believing that we're going to make money or get a great product, get more value than we're paying really fires up the dopamine delivery system. That's the second thing about dopamine. The third thing to remember is people hate randomness. If your presentation is confusing in any way, they try to solve that randomness with their own pattern fix. They will say, oh, I know what this is. This is a problem. If you did a great job and you gave them all the information as perfectly as it can be given, they go, I know what this is. This is great. They're done. They have all the information they need. There's no dopamine feeding into their mind. There's no fix coming to them. The meeting's over. I don't care if the meeting's gone on for 20 minutes and it's booked for two hours. You give them all the information they need. You complete the pattern. You solve the problem. There's no more dopamine. There's no drug fix. There's no euphoria coming. The meeting is over. But on the other side, if you created a problem that they can't solve, they will start to pattern make and try and solve it themselves with the limited information that they have. How do you win? You did a great job. The meeting's over. You did not a great job. They're going to pattern make, solve the problem themselves with the little information they have and the meeting is also over. How can this be? We're 20 minutes into it and the meeting is over. If you feed into either of these problems, you give them all the answers, it doesn't matter how hard you try to regain their attention. It's over. You pack your bags and you go home. There's clearly a solution for this or I wouldn't be talking about it. You have got to help them discover a new and bigger and better problem so they can earn a larger reward for their mind, get a kick of dopamine. So now in reveal the intrigue, you have to open a new area of inquiry that's related to your pitch. But you need to create arousal at this point. You need to activate the anticipation circuits of the mind. Anticipation circuitry forces us to play close attention to the possibility of coming rewards. All this background is leading us to understand that at the end of the pitch, they're tired, they are solving the problem that you have posed and they're basically ready to get out of the meeting. So you've got to recapture attention. And of course, this module is R, reveal the intrigue and you can tell by now we're going to recapture attention by dropping some new intrigue on them. But as a quick pause, there's some other ways to capture attention. They just work the way the mind likes to work. If you introduce high contrast ideas in a physical way, they will pay attention to you. Perception is not passive. You want people to see something clearly, give them the chance to move around it, to see how it interacts with other objects and people and ideas. Remember, we're very good at interpolating, getting small pieces of information, making patterns and coming up with answers from multiple sources. So we're not going to talk about specifically how to do that, but introducing high contrast ideas in a physical way will get people paying attention to you. The second thing you can do is introduce an object. Objects that abruptly appear get really fast attention. In general, our brain gives special treatment to objects as opposed to backgrounds and shadows and landscape. Think about it in an evolutionary way. People or animals or food require a response of some kind from the animal within us. So if you introduce an object, you're going to recapture attention at this point. I'm not proposing you do that. I'm not going to give you a method for doing that. It's just something to think about in context. A third thing you can do is if you introduce agency and motion, animacy, as it's called, to something, people will respond to it. We evolved in the world where animals and objects had to be paid attention to. Living things are more difficult and more dangerous objects than a rock. So our brains are biased in lots of ways to detect movement, what scientists call agency. So things that happen because someone wanted them to happen have to be paid attention to. So anytime you can introduce a sense of motion or agency to your pitch or your product or your idea, you're going to recapture attention. It's far bigger subject than we can address here, but it's important to recognize. High contrast ideas, objects abruptly appear and human-like or animal-like motion get attention. I just mentioned that because I want to now move into the last bit of context before we start doing some very specific things to develop intrigue. The reason we're so deeply in the subject of intrigue is that look at it this way. One, we have just given a pitch. Two, people are tired. Three, they are starting to make assumptions about the value of what we have. Four, they are asking questions to validate their assumptions. For other reasons too, but a lot of those questions are going to come to validate the assumptions that they're making. Number five, they are craving a dopamine hit and we have not given them a new way to get a dopamine kick. Number six, the meeting is coming to an end quickly and this is a problem. The cause of this problem is that people perceive patterns where none exist as we've said before and people leap to conclusions from fragments of information. It is important to understand how this came about. Why do we have this inside of us? So I want to say a little bit about our history as just a species. We as humans, we're hunter-gatherers, we lived in small nomadic bands, we're looking for people to mate with with most of our time, finding shelter or chasing things to eat. We spent a lot of our time avoiding predators and foraging for fruits, seeds, roots, whatever. Our earliest ancestors, the decisions they faced in daily life were very few and not very complex. Early man, what did he have to do? Avoid places where crocodiles and jaguars and piranha hang out. They had to figure out if it was going to rain for the day. They had to understand what kind of people would want to take their food away and try and kill them. So if you were early man, you would have to learn how to kill those people after you identified them and you had to try not to get it wrong very often. These are the basic tasks our brain evolved to perform. Find something to eat, find something to kill, find something to mate with, avoid piranha, look for rain and are the approaching people, friends or enemies. That's it. This is what we evolved to do. We're different from all other creatures in our ability to forecast, interpret, observe, correlate and understand cause and effect. Other things on this earth don't do that. Our human race is what, 200,000 years old and there hasn't been much additional development in that time. Just a quick aside, in 1997 the paleoanthropologists that do this kind of stuff, they found 154,000 year old skull, Homo sapiens skull in Ethiopia. It would have held what, 1,450 cubic centimeters in volume of brain and that's the same average as people living today. Not much development in 200,000 years. It's easy to visualize the ancient East African plain which we came from. I've said many times, we have dropped out of the trees onto the African savanna. That was a simple environment with alternating regions of sun and shade and dense green areas of trees and grass, vast open ground with small rivers that had dangerous sloping banks. I mean that's it. If this were a video game, it would be considered one of the worst ever. Too easy to figure out what to do next. Simple patterns clue you in in this environment how to adapt for survival. Once you get this environment right, food, shelter, friends, mates. You didn't need to know a lot more. You didn't go out and collect more information. Our ancestors learned to make the most of small samples of data and today our brains still work. This is what I call, I get it. Our brains work on very small samples and people go, I get it. They pattern make and fill in the rest of it. They don't need all the detail to make assumptions. People make decisions on very small samples of information. As I've said before, they perceive patterns where none exist and they leap to conclusions from fragments of information. We date back 200,000 years as I've said before but food, crops and the first cities only date back 11,000 years, okay, 5%. The earliest known financial markets in which products like rice and metal were traded were from Mesopotamia but the more formal markets and where trading of stocks and financial instruments happen, those are only 400 years old. We date back 200,000 years and the trading of financial instruments dates 400 years. If you can imagine all of human history as your 10 mile commute to work, the first financial transactions will not show up until your elevator ride to the fifth floor office. This is why our primitive brain finds economic calculation so difficult to focus on. It's great for solving prehistoric problems, simple patterns, strong emotional responses it does as fast, instantly and efficiently. We have 200,000 years of experience but we're doing those things. We only have 400 years of experience working on economics and financial transactions. The brain, the mind of the person you're pitching is not good at identifying randomness, organizing, complex ideas and making multivariate decisions. These are challenges our ancestors rarely faced but your customer has to do it the whole time he's dealing with you. They will perceive patterns where none exist and they will leap to conclusions from fragments of information and that is because there's 200,000 years of experience built into the mind of the person you're sitting across to do that. There is less than 400 years of experience in that person to understand the economic value proposition that you are proposing. You need their attention. You need their attention so they can slowly understand the economics and the value proposition of what you have. That takes time to get in their mind and time requires attention. Now that we understand why intrigue is so important, what the evolutionary biology basis for needing to use intrigue at the end of the pitch, we can move into applications of it. Why would you want intrigue? Of course because the person is tired, they're starting to make assumptions, they're pattern making and there's serious problems emerging. They're starting to ask questions and the social layer is breaking apart and the meeting is nearly over. That's why you want to use intrigue. So then it begs the question, what makes things intriguing? First of all, let's start with the basics. You, you're intriguing. You build celebrity status through your knowledge, your insight, your value. As many of you know, I help companies raise money and I'm different from most guys who do this. In this way, once I agree to take on a deal, I don't give up. I'm like a bulldog on a bone. I'm 35 for 35. One of those raises, I found $6 million in 30 days. That was great. Then in another one, it took me six months to find $5 million. I had to travel to Russia, to Switzerland, and Italy and in Russia, they do business very differently and I'd love to talk to you about that when we get some time together outside of this program. There is a lot of money in Russia and if you know how to get it, it's pretty easy. It took me about five months to figure out how it works there but once I did, it happened quickly. On that deal, I spent more than I made. That really hurt but what I have learned is when you take on a deal and you tell somebody you're going to do something, then you follow through to the end. Here's what else I learned. Sweet are the fruits of labor. You are an intriguing person. I think that's a, if I was to hear somebody say that to me, I would be intrigued. You can find your own methodology but you got to dig deep, find out what's interesting about you without bragging in a weird way and related in a social way that's also economic but you are intriguing. The second thing that's intriguing is to say, I know we're selling to you or we want your money or we want your purchase order or we want your investment but you actually can't have it until we authorize you. This is very common. You might say this transaction is a little different than some you might be familiar with. We have a pool of investors. There's five investors in our company and all the decisions about the company need to be unanimous. I have to carefully screen who we add as an investor pool to the company because we need all parties focused on the same financial outcome. If you have different goals from the other investors, we're not going to be able to make unanimous decisions. We're not going to be able to make unanimous decisions. So even if you want to invest, if you want to give me a check right now, it's not possible. I have to screen you and I have to authorize you so the other investors know that they're working in a peer group and when there are financial decisions to make, you're not going to be a bad apple. You're not going to be a problem. So yes, we want you to want to be in our deal but at the moment that you want it, we still have to get you authorized. There's a third way to develop intrigue and it's very common. And that is to say, hey, you know, you're not going to be a bad apple. We can only sell you our basic product. You cannot have the premium product. And actually that's quite common, right? You'll see many companies require you to buy the basic product before you can have the premium product. I mean, think about a snowboarding school. You can't just sign up for snowboarding and say, I want to go on the jumps and I want to go in the rail park. You've got to demonstrate that you can get down the bunny slope first and then you can get down the blue slopes and only then can you advance to do the cool stuff, stuff the instructors and the advanced students know how to do. You cannot pay them any amount of money to take you immediately to the rail park because you will die. They don't want that. So you cannot have the premium product. First you have to buy the basic product in my own business. It's the same way. If you want me to raise money for your deal, we need you to buy our basic advisory service. We've got to take you through that program so you understand how we work. It's only a couple thousand dollars. You've got to understand how we work and then if you want to move to real capital raising, we can advance. But we're not going to jump right into capital raising until you've bought our basic product because we don't know you well enough. So of course there is more product that you haven't seen. You can't buy our premium product until you buy our basic product. That is always intriguing because it lets you describe things that people can't have and it's very authentic. As you're building your own version of that, again think about the metaphor of the snowboarding school. Nobody is going to put you in a rail park until you've passed the earlier classes. This number three way to develop intrigue is very powerful. And I call this the, we're not sure that we're right for each other yet. This is similar to you can't have it but it's on a relationship basis. You can't have it means we have got to authorize you. We're not sure we're right for each other is I haven't authorized your personality. Not sure that we would get along. You might say something like this, we care about relationships with our customers and there are some things about you that we like for sure. We wouldn't stay here. We wouldn't be asking you to buy this. But there's some things, there's some things we're just not there yet. We're not sure about you. Because just because you're a buyer, it doesn't mean that we have to jump into a relationship with you because there's something called counterparty risk. So even if you buy from us, you can be a horrible customer and we can make no profit on you or we could lose money. You have customers yourself like that. I want to just do a little bit more work to understand you to make sure that not only is this product going to be great for you because that's what you want but you're going to be a great customer for us. I'm just not there yet. How does this work, this idea of counterparty? I'll give you an example. So let's say you sell your company sells MRI machines to hospitals. A hospital chain wants to order 500 but you don't have 500 MRI machines in stock. I mean that might be a $50 million inventory. So you have to build those. They better be a damn good customer because if they change that order after you've started building and ordered all the raw materials for 500 machines, you could lose millions. So buyers cannot be automatically customers. You have to think about them as a counterparty and if they're going to be a counterparty that can cause you damage, then you have to make damn sure it's a good relationship. Will the customer let you profit? Do they want you to profit? Are they easy to work with? Are they fun to work with? Are they additive to your company? Are they a good counterparty in the credit markets? If you really want to learn about counterparties, go to the internet and type in credit market counterparty. You'll see lots of descriptions. Buyers need to be good counterparty. It's not, you can't just take their money but they're bad people. So if they're acting up or they're trying to do things that are out of phase with the way you run your sales process, you can just say, hey, I'm not sure we're right for each other and we've got to explore this because even though you're buying what we have, you're also our counterparty and we need to make sure we have a great relationship. That is very intriguing to people that you would even say that. That's a great way to develop intrigue. It does other things as well but it's great for intrigue. The next point of intrigue is to deliver certainty and this is counterintuitive but certainty is very intriguing. Let me give you an example of Acme Software Company that I was helping raise money and we were able to offer investors a guaranteed 20%. Guaranteed 20% for a reason. The fund that was already invested in them could not invest any more money but they wanted to. So if somebody else invested, they would guarantee them a 20% return backed by $50 million in assets. So as much as anything can be a bond like guarantee, this was it at a 20% return. That was very intriguing. A lot of people wanted to look at that because there's many deals that say, hey, we'll give you a 20% return and there's many deals that will say we guarantee you a 20% return but there's very few that say we guarantee you a 20% return backed by assets. That certainty, if you think about it, becomes very intriguing. In another deal, and I'm going to show you that one specifically later, we were able to say next year's revenue is already under contract. Under contract, right? So $30 million of next year's revenue is already under contract. So most companies will say next year's projections is $30 million from our existing customers but very few can say that revenue is already under contract. That certainty, when we provided it in the description of the business, is what changed the face of the deal and then it became very easy to get meetings. If you want to think about certainty in an interesting way, in movies, right? We saw last year this movie called The Expendables. Look it up. It's an action movie. It's got Sylvester Stallone, Jason Statham, Jet Lee, Dolph Lungren, Randy Couture, the UFC fighter, Steve Austin, and Mickey Rourke in it. I mean, how can you go wrong? If you like action movies, that roster gives you certainty that some stuff is going to blow up, people are going to be shooting things and there's going to be mayhem. Certainty. That movie cost $80 million to make and it did $300 million in the box office. Certainty is very intriguing. There's another kind of certainty that's very powerful that I've found in my own experience and that's when you add certainty and celebrity. I'll show you this in practice now. Here is a pitch deck I built for the Bryan Brothers who is a world famous duo, the winningest duo in doubles tenants. They're amazing athletes and they've got one of the best records of all time. They have been starting a apparel business. This is the deck we built for them. What we found is that when we told people about the celebrity status of these athletes and we showed them their winning track record, even people that weren't familiar with them and we showed them that they were starting this tennis line of amazing tennis apparel, performance oriented tennis apparel, we found that when we told people about the Bryan Brothers and the reach of their platform, their celebrity status, but we also combined with the fact that they could call distributors and because of their celebrity, make distributors take their product. Make distributors take their product because their distributors were their friends and people that they knew very well. If you add certainty and celebrity together, then it became very interesting. There's all kinds of things you can do with celebrity. You can say, hey, use an investor in the Bryan Brothers business. We have to screen you because these are world class athletes. They're the all American winning guys. They're not just going to take any investor that's going to be a bad apple, so we really have to understand your experience as an investor and how you could add value to this business. These guys really care about relationships. So we have to see if the chemistry is good between us. You can do all kinds of things when you have certainty and celebrity. The Bryan Brothers have a brand and your behavior will reflect on their brand. So they really want to make sure that you're the right investor, but certainly because of their status and their far reaching platform and the millions of people that know them all over the world. The launch of this brand, of this apparel gear is very certain because again they're just going to call the distributors and say, hey, we're shipping you boxes. It's the Bryan Brothers. So adding certainty and celebrity together is very intriguing because people are obviously attracted to celebrity. They're attracted to certainty. You put those together and it becomes quite an amazing pull on people and certainly gets their attention. As I mentioned, certainty is a very powerful way to introduce intrigue. So I'm going to show you an example of how I used that before. This is a pitch deck I built for a company that we raised money for and it was very difficult subject. It's a genetics complex field. There's not a lot of investors for it. And so what we discovered, what I discovered is until we started providing certainty in the deal, until we started providing certainty, it was hard to get people to pay enough attention to the overall deal to absorb it. So on this page, the revenue summary is where we talked about the next year's revenues as being under contract. Now that seems like an obvious thing to do, but it wasn't at the time. But once I developed this single page, it changed the complete finance ability of the whole company. It seemed obvious when we said, hey, there's customer contracts. But when I built this page like this and said, the next year's revenue are under contract by these customers, it was intriguing enough to get people to pay enough attention to the deal to absorb all the other complex aspects about it. Certainty can be very intriguing. As we continue to talk about applications and intrigue, I'm going to show you another pitch deck that falls under the you are intriguing subject. And I think we provide this. Well, I know we provide this as part of the Pitch Mastery program and you can download this and look at it. You are intriguing. In this case, we built this pitch deck for a wealth manager and we made the whole deck about Alex and what's interesting about him. Now Alex has a very interesting story that this deck communicates. You'll see it within it. And certainly when he gives his pitch in person. So he was a paramedic and he ended up saving a young woman's life. It's an amazing story when you hear him tell it. And he came to really care because of his family about the financial well-being of physicians. And so he left a career that he was very good at and something he enjoyed to go into this wealth management for physicians and he's an expert in it and he focuses on it and his family is in it. And he has done some amazing things to celebrityize himself. He's told his company he's not going to take hundreds of clients. Like they ask him to do. But just focus on less than 50 clients in a specific field. So this whole deck, instead of being focused on a company, is focused on an individual. And I recommend you go through it. It's a great example how to celebrityize yourself and make yourself interesting. A lot of are or reveal the intrigue. This module has been about understanding the role of intrigue in the overall pitch process and why at the end of the pitch of the telling the story you have to be so focused on developing intrigue. Because if at the beginning I just said, hey, at the end of the pitch you need to deploy some intrigue, it wouldn't have been deeply felt. But now you understand why it's so important. It's time to talk about action steps and how to actually do it. The first thing in developing intrigue has actually nothing to do with intrigue. You have got to be willing to walk away from the deal. You have got to be willing to walk away from the room or the phone call or the internet site transaction. Got to be willing to walk away inside yourself. If you don't, it's going to be a boring meeting. Your neediness is going to be cloying. We've talked about this in prizing. We talked about this in framing. We talked about this and tell the story. We're talking about it again in revealing intrigue. You have got to be willing to walk away. If you don't have this fundamental skill inside of you, then everything else is going to be built in castles, castles on sand. You're not going to have a strong foundation. The foundation is the willingness to walk away and to signal and many times across the presentation that if it's not right for you, you will take your prize, your toys, and go play in a different sandbox. It's very important. I'll give you a quick example. I was pitching a company on handling some capital raising for them last week. They asked me what my fees are. I said my fees are reasonable. They're contended to even be below market, but they're definitely at market. I learned from a partner of mine many years ago, even though because of my skill set, I can charge high fees higher than anybody else. I learned not to be a pig because John would say, hey, Orrin, don't be a pig. I say, John, we can charge 10% even though market is seven on the fees for this deal. We can get 3% more than anybody else can get. You say, Orrin, don't be a pig. Leave some money on the table for other people. Don't load the deal. Take a fair fee and leave money on the table. I said to the guys I was pitching, they said, Orrin, what are your fees? I said, well, I learned many years ago from this guy, John, not to be a pig and we charge you reasonable fees, five to 7% on equity, 1% on debt. He says to me, well, I negotiate hard because I'm a pig. I want as much as I can get for myself. That's what the guy on the other side of the table, the VP of finance says. So I had to signal to him that I was willing to walk away. I said, if that's how you feel, we would not work well together and I would not take this deal on. If you really felt that way and that's how you treat deals and you don't mind taking more than your fair share and you don't want to leave anything on the table for other people, I would never want to work with you and I wouldn't want to do this deal. And of course, 10 seconds later, he came back and said, Hey, I'm just kidding. You know, you were talking about being a pig and I was just kidding about all that. That's not how I am. I value relationships and everything like that. So you have to be able to signal your willingness to walk away. That's the fundamental action step or foundation to be able to deliver intrigue or to do any pitch, but it's not to be overt about it. You can't at every minute say, Hey, I'm willing to walk away. I'm willing to walk away. If you don't do what I want, I'm willing to walk away. You've got to signal this willingness at the right time, especially when people are out of phase with your values. You could say, Hey, that wouldn't work for us. This is not a deal we would do. The next thing that you have to do is you have to identify and collect all the resources you have and buy resources. I mean the valuable, insightful knowledge that you have about the market and the product and the idea and not stuff that's well known or in the common body of knowledge. It's got to be real insight and the insight has to be useful and has to be actionable and has to be interesting. The reason I'm asking you to collect all of this information and resources is because there's a worksheet that is going to help you build your intrigue specifically, but you need to know what insightful knowledge you have. Then within the worksheet, you'll get a specific script on how to reveal things that are intriguing at the end of your presentation or your pitch. Another thing you want to think about is what risk can a customer give you? What risk can a customer give you? How can a customer be a risky counterparty? Can they place a giant order like Walmart? Can be a risky, great customer, but risky counterparty because they can order more stuff than you can manufacture. If you sell toothbrushes and Walmart gives you an order for a million five and your suppliers can't get you that much, you have a problem. What risks can your customers give you? What problems can your customers cause you? In the worksheet, you'll see how to take those risks and specifically develop intrigue out of them. The next thing is what aspects of your product or service are for power users? If you recall, you said one way to develop intrigue is to say you can't have the part of the product that's for power users. You have to start with the basic one. In order to figure out how to provide that intrigue, you have to answer the question what aspects of your product or service are for power users? The next thing that you have to decide and think about, and this will be in the worksheet as well, is what signs of incompatibility do you look for in people? If they're rude, if they want to rush the presentation, if they're asking too many analytical questions, if they're moving too slow, too fast, too fat, too skinny, they don't like you, you don't like them, what signs of incompatibility do you look for? Because it's very intriguing to say, I'm not sure we're compatible yet and we need to explore that some more. In the worksheet, we'll give you a specific script how to do that, but you have got to look for those signs of incompatibility. The next thing is what's cool about what you do? What's cool about what you do? Obviously, things that are cool are intriguing. In some ways, I think about I have a cool car, and when they sold it to me, they said, hey, there's some super cool things about this car. The subtext is that's intriguing. I know you'll never probably use it, but at 80 miles an hour, this wing pops out and it stabilizes the car. It's very cool. At the point that I was getting cool and buying that car, what did they do? They said, let me show you some cool stuff. You're going to think about your product in terms of what's cool about what you do, or what's cool about the idea, or what's cool about the product. When things start to cool off at the end of your pitch, you're going to bring that in, the cool factor which is intriguing. The worksheet will give you a specific script for doing that, but you have to bring the raw material. Again, step one, identify and collect all your resources. What's valuable and insightful? What knowledge do you have that's true insight about your idea or your industry, your product, your service, your business? Number two, what risks can a customer give you? What counterparty risks can they provide? Number three, what aspects of your product are for power users? What's the equivalent of the snowboard rail park in your business? Number four, what signs of incompatibility do you look for in other people? It's very intriguing to people when you say, I'm not sure I would do business with you even if you handed me a check. We may be incompatible because of this reason. What signs of incompatibility do you look for? Number five, what's cool about what you do? I mean really cool, not fake cool. I'm just going to give you a quick graphic to let you know what's ahead and where we've come from, where we are, and where we're going. In the broader scheme of things, we set the frame and we told the story because we wanted attention. At the end of the pitch, some things are happening that we have to be concerned about. We've given the pitch, people are tired. They're starting to make assumptions about the value of what we have. They are now going to ask questions and these questions are going to destroy or break down the social layer. They're asking questions to validate the assumptions they're making about you. They are craving the audience or the buyer, people you're talking to, pitching to. They're craving another dopamine fix and we haven't given them away at the end of the pitch. We haven't given them away to get a new dopamine kick. The meeting is coming to the end. That's a problem and that's why we have to reveal some intrigue at this point. Next, you're going to download the worksheet and really find ways to do that specifically for your business. We're going to help you to the extent we can, but you're going to start with the worksheet. What's coming next is how to offer the prize. That's very exciting because now you'll be moving from engagement into decision. Certainly when we nail the help point, we're going to be at the decision mode and get the deal is obviously when money transacts. There's still work to do to get money to transact even if you've created a great desire. They want the product. You have nailed the hook point. You still got to get money to transact. We still have some work to do, but we're very far from getting a deal closed. Download the worksheet now and then we'll see you next on the next module which is offer the prize.
